caveats: a: The occupation table covers all of NAICS 2213 and is dominated by water and wastewater systems rather than thermal utilities.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not reveal automation already embedded in plant controls.
a: Staffing varies materially between steam, hot-water, chilled-water, combined heat and power, and geothermal networks.
rho: DOE projects demonstrate technical potential rather than representative commercial adoption.
rho: Optimization can reduce fuel rather than labor, which is outside this primitive unless it also substitutes work or avoids hiring.
rho: Boiler requirements differ by fuel, source category, location, and equipment configuration.
e: EIA's system universe is not a NAICS firm universe and includes captive campus and government systems.
e: A single operator may own multiple plants or networks, while a system can involve several legal entities.
e: The provided firm count is itself margin-bridged from size classes, and capital-intensive utility margins can vary sharply by contract and regulation.
s5: The Census age evidence is cross-industry, represents responding employer-business owners, and uses 2018 data.
s5: Asset or concession transfers may not constitute acquisition of a transferable operating firm.
s5: Public, captive, and large corporate systems have transfer mechanisms unrelated to individual owner age.
q: Con Edison is far larger than the screened band and operates in a unique dense urban market.
q: Rate regulation and contract structures differ by state, municipality, ownership model, and customer class.
q: A local network can have strong physical exclusivity while still facing political or anchor-customer pressure on rates.
d5: The central growth observation covers 2012 through 2016 and may not represent the next five years.
d5: Connected floorspace is not constant-quality thermal output and heating and cooling floorspace can overlap.
d5: EIA commercial-building data include captive district heat and do not isolate screened private suppliers.
o: Operator identity can change even when the physical thermal network remains in use.
o: Networked geothermal preserves an operator role but can materially change plant, staffing, and revenue models.
o: Remote monitoring can centralize accountability across multiple systems without eliminating it.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are typically commercial buildings, hospitals, universities, public institutions, residential complexes, industrial sites, and mixed-use districts. Revenue commonly combines usage or demand charges with recurring customer charges under regulated tariffs or long-term service contracts, sometimes including fuel or other cost adjustments.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive internal units, non-control financing vehicles, municipal or government-owned systems, campus central plants serving only affiliated buildings, in-building HVAC contractors, equipment wholesalers, firms outside the screened EBITDA band, and entities that own passive infrastructure without accountable thermal-service operations are excluded.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly supply steam, hot water, heated air, or chilled water from a central plant through a thermal network to multiple external customer buildings, and that retain accountable responsibility for plant, distribution, metering, reliability, compliance, and customer service.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment shares in the broader NAICS 2213 water, sewage, and other systems population, not wage-weighted tasks in NAICS 221330 or current task automation. I translate its substantial office, management, production, and maintenance shares into exposure for billing, forecasting, reporting, engineering support, dispatch, and predictive-maintenance workflows while discounting hands-on plant and network work. Water and sewage operations likely have more field and treatment labor than some thermal systems, biasing the exposed share downward for highly automated district-energy plants but upward if thermal operators require more skilled stationary-engineer coverage.
caveats: The occupation table covers all of NAICS 2213 and is dominated by water and wastewater systems rather than thermal utilities.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not reveal automation already embedded in plant controls.
Staffing varies materially between steam, hot-water, chilled-water, combined heat and power, and geothermal networks.
a.change_evidence: Obtain NAICS 221330 payroll by detailed occupation and conduct time-and-task studies across at least fifteen screened plants, separating current automation from remaining work in control rooms, engineering, field maintenance, metering, billing, compliance, and customer service.
a.rationale: AI can reduce work in meter-data validation, billing exceptions, load and weather forecasting, fuel scheduling, maintenance triage, compliance drafting, work-order administration, customer communication, engineering document search, and operating-log analysis. Continuous plant supervision, valve and pipe work, equipment repair, emergency response, physical inspection, and accountable operating decisions remain substantially human.

d5.bridge: EIA's market characterization reports historical growth in connected district-energy floorspace and a technology mix trend, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket of screened private firms. Applying the reported annual floorspace growth to five years yields the central quantity direction, while the interval allows for weather, building efficiency, customer additions and losses, steam-to-hot-water conversion, cooling growth, and a much older observation period. Floorspace can overstate thermal quantity when intensity falls and understate value when cooling or resilience services expand.
caveats: The central growth observation covers 2012 through 2016 and may not represent the next five years.
Connected floorspace is not constant-quality thermal output and heating and cooling floorspace can overlap.
EIA commercial-building data include captive district heat and do not isolate screened private suppliers.
d5.change_evidence: Collect weather-normalized billed thermal units, connected floorspace, peak demand, customer additions and disconnections, efficiency retrofits, conversion projects, and signed pipeline by system; compare current International District Energy Association data with EIA's historical baseline.
d5.rationale: Heating and cooling are persistent building needs, and EIA evidence shows a large installed base with modest historical connected-space growth. New district-scale geothermal and thermal-network configurations can add customers, while building efficiency, mild weather, individual heat pumps, customer self-generation, steam-system contraction, and redevelopment can reduce incumbent output. Cooling and mixed thermal networks may grow faster than legacy steam.

caveats: EIA's system universe is not a NAICS firm universe and includes captive campus and government systems.
A single operator may own multiple plants or networks, while a system can involve several legal entities.
The provided firm count is itself margin-bridged from size classes, and capital-intensive utility margins can vary sharply by contract and regulation.
e.change_evidence: Reconcile SUSB firms to EIA and International District Energy Association system lists, then verify ownership, external-customer share, standalone employees, normalized EBITDA, control rights, regulated status, and plant-versus-passive-asset responsibility for every candidate.
e.rationale: External-customer district utilities clearly fit the recurring-service lens, but much of the physical system universe consists of municipal, military, hospital, university, or other campus plants that are captive or government owned. The remaining private universe also includes large utility subsidiaries, firms outside the EBITDA band, joint ventures, and asset-holding structures without standalone accountable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221330 ($153,159,854) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221330 ($1,262,360,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $733,930 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221330); class <5: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,880,900 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,006,010 -> IN $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,605,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $560,582 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,549,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,286,911 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,911,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,905,421 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $165,274,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,713,820 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,269,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,665,135 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 32

caveats: Operator identity can change even when the physical thermal network remains in use.
Networked geothermal preserves an operator role but can materially change plant, staffing, and revenue models.
Remote monitoring can centralize accountability across multiple systems without eliminating it.
o.change_evidence: Track customer bypass and disconnection rates, self-generation economics, municipalization, in-building heat-pump conversions, outsourced operations contracts, remote-operation staffing, and ownership changes in plant and distribution responsibility for each eligible network.
o.rationale: Most surviving district thermal quantity requires an accountable operator because a central plant, pipe network, pumps, valves, meters, building interfaces, emissions duties, maintenance, and emergency response cannot be replaced by software alone. Quantity can leave the lens when customers install individual equipment, campuses self-operate, systems municipalize, buildings electrify independently, or service shifts to a passive network with operations absorbed by a different accountable entity.

q.bridge: Con Edison's regulated New York steam tariff shows customer and usage charges plus fuel adjustments for one very large urban system, not the share of AI-enabled gross benefit retained by lower-middle-market operators nationwide. I use it to establish that tariffs can explicitly pass selected costs and subject rates to regulatory review, then widen for unregulated bilateral contracts and differing rate structures. The regulated example may understate retention where private contracts fix prices, but may overstate it where open-book contracts share efficiencies.
caveats: Con Edison is far larger than the screened band and operates in a unique dense urban market.
Rate regulation and contract structures differ by state, municipality, ownership model, and customer class.
A local network can have strong physical exclusivity while still facing political or anchor-customer pressure on rates.
q.change_evidence: Review tariffs, rate-case testimony, fuel-adjustment clauses, private service agreements, renewal records, shared-savings provisions, customer concentration, avoided-cost alternatives, and historical operating-expense disallowances for representative eligible systems.
q.rationale: High switching costs, embedded pipes, reliability obligations, and local network exclusivity support retention under fixed-price contracts or between rate cases. Regulated cost-of-service reviews, fuel riders, customer benchmarking, renewal negotiations, shared-savings provisions, and large anchor-customer leverage can return part of an implemented benefit to customers. Savings from non-labor fuel optimization should not be counted as the benefit being retained here.

rho.bridge: DOE documents that many community district-energy systems use only basic control optimization and is funding improved control architectures and algorithms, which measures technical headroom in system optimization rather than five-year implementation of labor-substituting AI at screened firms. I translate that headroom to workflow implementation while discounting for legacy control systems, sparse labeled failure data, cybersecurity, uptime, safety, emissions, capital approval, and mandatory human accountability. Research pilots likely overstate near-term adoption at small operators.
caveats: DOE projects demonstrate technical potential rather than representative commercial adoption.
Optimization can reduce fuel rather than labor, which is outside this primitive unless it also substitutes work or avoids hiring.
Boiler requirements differ by fuel, source category, location, and equipment configuration.
rho.change_evidence: Survey screened operators on deployed AI by workflow, control-system architecture, cybersecurity review, capital cost, exception rates, operator override frequency, outage and safety performance, and realized labor hours; obtain before-and-after records from advanced-control installations.
rho.rationale: Metering, weather, plant telemetry, maintenance histories, contracts, and fuel data can support prediction and decision support, but many systems have fragmented legacy controls and limited integration. Operators will adopt faster in planning, reporting, billing, and advisory maintenance than in closed-loop control because thermal service is continuous, failures affect many occupied buildings, and boiler and emissions obligations require auditable procedures.

s5.bridge: Census measures owner ages across responding US employer businesses in 2018, not qualifying control transfers among private district-energy operators. I translate the older-owner prevalence into a five-year transfer probability, then discount for corporate, infrastructure-fund, joint-venture, and utility ownership; internal succession; recapitalizations; and asset sales without a transferable operating company. The broad small-business population likely overstates age-driven succession for capital-intensive utilities.
caveats: The Census age evidence is cross-industry, represents responding employer-business owners, and uses 2018 data.
Asset or concession transfers may not constitute acquisition of a transferable operating firm.
Public, captive, and large corporate systems have transfer mechanisms unrelated to individual owner age.
s5.change_evidence: Map beneficial owners, ownership tenure, fund vintage, debt maturity, concession terms, succession plans, and control-change provisions for the eligible universe; obtain closed thermal-infrastructure transactions from PitchBook, Inframation, IJGlobal, state commission dockets, and sector advisers.
s5.rationale: A limited subset of eligible systems may be founder- or family-controlled and face succession, while infrastructure investors and strategic utilities can transact for long-duration contracted assets. Transfer frequency is constrained by local regulation, customer consent, municipal franchise terms, environmental liabilities, complex project finance, and the small verified firm universe.

principal_driver: Long-lived connected thermal networks preserve accountable recurring demand while data-rich administrative and operating-support workflows offer implementable productivity gains.
principal_weakness: Few systems are simultaneously private, externally serving, lower-middle-market, independently transferable, and able to retain efficiencies after regulation or contract renewal.

caveats: The a and rho bases combine a broader NAICS 221300 employment mix with DOE technical-control headroom; neither source measures wage-weighted automatable tasks or five-year labor-substituting implementation at screened 221330 firms.

The eligible-firm share e is an explicitly labeled estimate. The cited system and commercial-building universes establish the need for exclusions but do not empirically identify the private, external-customer, lower-middle-market operator share.
The s5 proxy translates 2018 owner ages for responding employer businesses across all industries into transfer probability without district-energy ownership or transaction evidence.

The q estimate rests on one large regulated New York steam utility; contract structures and efficiency-sharing for lower-middle-market private systems remain unobserved despite the wide disclosed range.

The d5 base compounds a 2012-2016 connected-floorspace growth rate from a 2018 report. Floorspace is not weather-normalized thermal quantity, and the evidence does not isolate screened private suppliers.

The o value is an explicit estimate: the sources establish persistent physical networks and some compliance duties, but they do not measure customer bypass, municipalization, outsourced operations, or the fraction of future quantity retaining a qualifying accountable operator.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 Census NAICS definition supports the industry boundary, including steam, heated-air, and cooled-air supply and distribution through mains; it is appropriately used as a boundary anchor rather than as direct evidence for the estimated eligible share.

The May 2023 OEWS table reports the stated occupation shares for NAICS 221300, including management, office and administrative support, installation and maintenance, and production. The packet clearly discloses that this is a broader-industry employment proxy rather than a wage-weighted task measure for 221330.

DOE describes central-plant district systems and pipe networks and explicitly states that most current community-based systems employ only basic controls optimization while presenting an advanced-controls R&D project. This supports technical headroom, not representative labor implementation, as the bridge acknowledges.

The 2018 EIA-commissioned characterization covers a 660-system IDEA database, documents campus and downtown settings, and reports approximately 0.65 percent annual connected-floorspace growth for 2012-2016 plus a transition toward hot water and cooling. The packet accurately flags the old period, system-versus-firm mismatch, and floorspace-versus-output limitation.

The 2018 CBECS summary supports the stated district-heat building, consumption, spending, floorspace, and space-heating statistics. It establishes scale and end use but does not isolate private lower-middle-market operators or directly measure eligibility or five-year demand growth.

The Census graphic supports the stated 51 percent age-55-and-over figure for responding owners of employer businesses using 2018 data and expressly warns that the result does not represent all US owners. The cross-industry transfer-probability translation is disclosed as a proxy.

EPA supports the existence of notification, electronic reporting, energy-assessment, tune-up, recordkeeping, and emissions obligations for covered area-source boilers. Its fuel, source-category, and equipment exemptions mean it does not establish uniform obligations across all screened thermal systems.

Con Edison's PSC No. 4 steam materials show regulated tariffs, customer and usage charges, fuel adjustments, and delivery revenue surcharges. This supports the stated example of a regulated billing and pass-through structure, with the packet appropriately limiting the inference to one unusually large urban system.

DOE describes networked geothermal serving neighborhoods, blocks, campuses, and communities through shared piping, heat pumps, and heat exchangers. It supports continued networked thermal service under technology substitution but does not directly measure the numerical demand or accountable-operator shares.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily providing steam, heated air, or cooled air and notes that steam may be distributed through mains; distinguishes HVAC installation and equipment wholesaling.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 221330 Steam and Air-Conditioning Supply

sources.supports: Provides the broader water, sewage, and other systems occupation mix, including 23.11 percent office and administrative support, 32.13 percent production, 12.88 percent installation and maintenance, and 9.48 percent management.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 221300

sources.supports: Describes central plants and pipe networks, efficiency and resilience benefits, emerging control and optimization tools, and a DOE project statement that many community systems use only basic control optimization.
sources.title: Combined Heat and Power and District Energy

sources.supports: Analyzes 660 US district-energy systems, their campus and downtown customer settings, central plants and networks, fuel mix, historical connected floorspace, reported annual additions, and trends toward modest growth, hot water, and cooling.
sources.title: U.S. District Energy Services Market Characterization

sources.supports: Reports 86,000 commercial buildings using district heat, 305 trillion Btu consumed, 4.3 billion dollars spent, 7 percent of commercial floorspace using it, and 89 percent of district heat consumption used for space heating in 2018.
sources.title: Energy Sources Used in U.S. Commercial Buildings

sources.supports: Reports that 51 percent of responding US employer-business owners were at least age 55, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey using 2018 data.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Explains affected boiler fuels and exemptions, notification and electronic compliance reporting, performance testing, energy assessments, tune-ups, recordkeeping, emissions, and operator compliance obligations under the area-source boiler rule.
sources.title: Compliance for Industrial, Commercial, and Institutional Area Source Boilers

sources.supports: Shows a regulated steam schedule with customer and usage rates, fuel adjustments, delivery revenue surcharges, and Public Service Commission tariffs, illustrating one important industry billing and pass-through model.
sources.title: Steam Rates and Tariffs

sources.supports: Describes networked geothermal systems serving neighborhoods, blocks, campuses, and communities through connected piping, heat pumps, and heat exchangers, supporting technology substitution within an operator-required network model.
sources.title: Geothermal Heating and Cooling

title: Steam and Air-Conditioning Supply
